statements.statement: I keep a mental tally now of what I eat and who I spend time with, because I can feel the difference by mid-afternoon. If I have lunch with my colleague who complains nonstop, I'm foggy and irritable until dinner, but after a quiet walk I can think clearly again.

When I'm scrolling my phone before bed, I notice I'm choosing the blurry feeling—like I'm deliberately putting static between myself and whatever's actually there. I can see I'm doing it, but I also see I could stop.

My mornings have three versions depending on whether I wake up restless, sluggish, or clear, and I'm learning which breakfast and which music shift me toward the clear one. It's not automatic, but I can see the pattern now and I'm starting to steer it.

After certain meetings I feel wound tight and can't sit still, after others I'm drained and want to nap, and after a few I feel sharp and light. I'm tracking which people and topics do what, because I can feel myself being shaped by the room.

When my kids are fighting and I'm already running on coffee and no sleep, I notice I'm operating from some frantic place that makes everything worse. If I stop and eat something real, the whole atmosphere shifts—not just in me, in the room.

I used to think my mood was just random, but now I see it follows what I ate yesterday, how much I moved, and whether I spent the evening watching TV or reading. It's like I'm picking the lens I'll see through tomorrow, and I'm starting to pick on purpose.

At my sister's wedding I could feel myself getting pulled into the chaos—everyone rushing, no one listening, everything loud. I stepped outside for ten minutes and when I came back the noise was still there but I wasn't caught in it anymore.

I notice there's a quality underneath my thoughts that changes depending on whether I'm pushing too hard, zoning out, or actually present. I'm learning to check which one I'm in before I make decisions, because they each give me different answers.

When I'm with my partner late at night after a long day, I can tell if I'm talking from the tired, dull place or the wound-up, defensive place. I'm starting to notice it while it's happening, not just afterward, and sometimes I can shift gears mid-conversation.

I used to think something was either wrong with me or wrong with the world, but now I see there's this veil that gets thicker or thinner depending on what I do. When it's thin I can see what's actually there, and when it's thick everything feels off but nothing's really changed.

My mother-in-law insists on heavy food when I visit, and by evening I feel slow and my thoughts are muddy, but after the temple morning walk I'm clear again. I'm learning to balance what she needs me to eat with what keeps me steady, and I can feel the difference hour by hour.

I notice now whether I'm in the restless mode where I snap at the housemaid, the dull mode where I can't finish anything, or the clear mode where I actually see what needs doing. My grandmother taught me to watch this, and now I choose my morning routine to tip toward the clear one.

When extended family gathers for lunch and everyone's talking over each other, I can feel myself getting either agitated or shut down, but if I had a light breakfast and sat quietly beforehand I can stay centered. It's like I'm choosing which film covers my eyes before I even walk into the room.

I keep track now of which relatives leave me feeling heavy, which leave me scattered, and which leave me light. It's not about whether I like them—it's something in the quality of the time, and I'm learning to notice it so I can adjust the rest of my day.

My father insists on late, rich dinners and then wonders why I'm irritable in the mornings. I've started eating less at night and going for an early walk, and I can see the fog lifting—not dramatically, but enough that I recognize myself again by mid-morning.

During festival preparations when everyone's rushing and arguing about details, I notice I get pulled into either the frantic energy or the numb exhaustion. If I pause and drink something warm and sit for five minutes, I can feel a third option open up—clearer, steadier, like the noise is still there but I'm not made of it.

I used to think my mood was just how I am, but my aunt showed me it follows what I eat, who I spend time with, and whether I watch television or read sutras before bed. Now I'm choosing deliberately, and I can feel the curtain between me and what's real getting lighter or heavier depending on yesterday's choices.

At work when my boss is in a driving mood the whole office gets tense, and I can feel myself either racing to keep up or shutting down completely. I'm learning to eat a proper lunch and step outside, and when I come back I can see the pressure is still there but I'm not tangled in it the same way.

When my children fight in the evening and I've been snacking on fried things all afternoon, I'm short-tempered and everything feels harder than it is. If I had vegetable soup and moved my body earlier, the same fight doesn't stick to me—I can still see them clearly instead of through this haze of irritation.

I notice there's something covering what I can actually perceive, and it shifts depending on whether I'm agitated, dull, or calm. My practice now is just watching which state I'm in moment to moment, and making small adjustments—less oil, more quiet, different company—so the covering thins and I can see what was always underneath.

I've started tracking every meal in a notebook—not calories, but whether it made me feel sharp or sluggish or wired. By Thursday I'm spending more time analyzing what I ate than actually tasting anything, and my partner says I've turned dinner into a self-improvement project.

When my manager gives feedback in our one-on-one, I'm barely listening to what she's saying because I'm already cataloging whether I'm being defensive or spacey or overly agreeable. I leave the meeting exhausted, like I've been watching myself perform instead of just having the conversation.

I can't get through a workout without monitoring whether I'm pushing from ambition or discipline or just going through the motions. The gym used to clear my head—now I'm mentally filing every rep into categories, and I've lost whatever I went there to find.

My best friend called upset about her breakup, and while she was talking I caught myself diagnosing which state I was in—am I really present or am I just pretending to care? By the time I tuned back in, she'd moved on to something else and I have no idea what I missed.

I keep a mental tally of whether my morning coffee is making me productive or just anxious, and by 10am I've already spent more energy analyzing the caffeine than doing any actual work. My to-do list sits untouched while I recalibrate my inner weather report.

On the drive home I replay every interaction from the day, sorting them into piles—was I clear-headed in that email, scattered in the team meeting, dull during lunch? I pull into the driveway and realize I don't remember a single turn I took.

I've started declining weekend plans based on whether I think I'll be in the right state to enjoy them, but the calculus has gotten so complicated that I just stay home most Saturdays. My friends have stopped inviting me, and I tell myself it's because I'm being discerning.

When my kid asks me to play after school, there's this whole internal audit—am I too revved up, too tired, too distracted? By the time I've sorted through it, he's already wandered off to his room, and I'm left holding the Lego box wondering what just happened.

I used to love cooking, but now I research which ingredients are supposed to make me calmer or sharper, and I stand in the grocery aisle paralyzed by whether the chicken or the lentils will put me in a better state tomorrow. Dinner has become a strategy session I can't win.

I lie in bed running diagnostics on why I can't fall asleep—was it the afternoon coffee, the tense phone call, the fact that I skipped my walk? I'm so busy categorizing the reasons I'm awake that I'm still awake at 2am, and the whole thing has become its own problem.

I've started keeping notes on which family members leave me feeling light or heavy after we talk, and now I can barely have a conversation with my sister without monitoring my own energy levels. Last week she asked if I was even listening, and I realized I'd been too busy tracking my reactions to actually respond.

My mother-in-law brings over food every Sunday, and instead of just eating it I'm cataloging whether the fried things are making me sluggish or the ginger is sharpening me up. She's noticed I've stopped finishing my plate, and now there's this tension at the table I can't explain without sounding ungrateful.

At the office I watch myself in meetings, sorting every comment I make into whether it came from clarity or restlessness or fog. By lunch I'm so drained from the self-observation that I eat at my desk in silence, and my colleagues have stopped asking if I want to join them.

During festival preparations I'm supposed to be helping my aunties in the kitchen, but I'm mentally tracking whether chopping vegetables is grounding me or just making me more mechanical. I miss half of what they're saying, and they've started exchanging looks like I've become difficult.

I've begun judging which cousins to sit with at gatherings based on whether their energy will pull me into dullness or agitation, and now I spend the whole evening calculating seating arrangements instead of relaxing. My uncle asked why I've gotten so standoffish, and I had no answer that wouldn't sound strange.

My father asks me to drive him to the temple on Saturday mornings, and I'm running an internal checklist the whole way—am I present enough for this, or am I just going through motions? By the time we arrive I'm so tangled in self-assessment that the prayer feels like another task I'm failing.

When neighbors drop by unannounced, I'm immediately analyzing whether I have the steadiness to host them or if I'm too scattered, and they can see me hesitating at the door. Word has gotten around that I've become odd, and the visits have mostly stopped.

I used to enjoy the evening walk to the market, but now I'm monitoring every step—is this calming me or am I just numb? I come home with half the groceries forgotten because I was too busy reading my own state to remember why I went.

My daughter wants help with her homework after dinner, but I'm doing this whole internal scan of whether I'm clear-headed enough to explain fractions or if I'm still carrying the weight of the day. She's started asking her grandmother instead, and I can see the hurt in her face when I hesitate.

At night I lie awake sorting through every meal, every conversation, every commute, trying to trace which inputs pushed me toward fog or frenzy. My husband has stopped asking what's wrong because I can't explain that I've turned my whole day into a diagnostic I can't shut off.

I wake up already irritated some mornings and can't figure out why, then by noon I'm so tired I can barely keep my eyes open at my desk. It's like I'm cycling through different versions of myself and none of them feel like me driving.

When my wife asks if I want Thai or Italian for dinner, I realize I've been making that choice based on whatever mood hit me when she asked, not on what I actually want. By the time we're eating I'm already somewhere else entirely, scrolling my phone without tasting the food.

After three cups of coffee I'm sharp in the morning meeting, talking fast and interrupting people, but I don't notice I'm doing it until my manager pulls me aside. By 3pm I'm so foggy I have to reread the same email four times, and I have no idea what shifted.

I'll be fine all week and then Saturday hits and I can't get off the couch, just watching TV for six hours straight. It feels like something outside me decides whether I'm wired or dead, and I'm just along for the ride.

My daughter asks me to play and some days I'm all energy, building forts and running around, other days I can barely look up from my phone. I don't choose it—it's like I wake up and find out which dad showed up today.

At the gym I'm either pushing every set to failure, feeling invincible, or I walk in and immediately want to leave, like my body's made of lead. I never know which one I'll be until I'm already there.

When I'm with my college friends I get loud and competitive, talking over everyone, but with my brother's family I just sit there feeling dull and checked out. It's like different rooms pull different people out of me and I don't have a say.

Some mornings I'm out the door early, answering emails before breakfast, feeling like I could run the whole company. Other mornings I hit snooze five times and show up late, moving through the day like I'm underwater, and I have no idea what the difference is.

I'll go from calm and present during my commute to suddenly anxious and snapping at my partner the second I walk in the door. It's like I'm a different person every few hours and nobody told me the schedule.

After a work dinner with certain colleagues I feel sharp and full of ideas, after others I'm so tired I need to go straight to bed. I think it's just luck, like catching a cold—I don't see that I'm choosing which version of myself to feed every time I say yes.

My mother-in-law serves me lunch and some days I eat three plates, feeling energized and talkative, other days the same food makes me so heavy I need to lie down immediately. I never connect it to what I ate for breakfast or how I spent the morning—it just happens to me.

At the office I'll be focused and polite all morning, then after a certain colleague stops by my desk I'm agitated for hours, snapping at junior staff. I don't notice that his restless energy moved into me—I just think I'm having a bad afternoon.

When my father starts talking about family obligations at dinner, something in me goes dull and I stop being able to follow the conversation. By the end of the meal I'm so foggy I can't even remember what was decided, like a cloud just settled over me.

I wake up before dawn some days, my mind racing with plans, texting people before they're even awake, then two days later I can't get out of bed until noon. It feels like the weather—something that just happens to me, not something I have any part in.

My wife makes me eat hot soup when I'm feeling scattered and it works, but I don't connect the dots—I just think I got lucky and the day turned around. If she's away and I eat cold leftovers for three days, I'm irritable and confused and blame it on work stress.

After my aunt's puja I feel clear and steady for a few hours, but then I go out drinking with friends and wake up the next day so heavy and dull I can't think straight. I treat them like unrelated events, not like I'm swinging between different states all the time.

When I'm with my older cousins I become loud and boastful, interrupting everyone, but with my grandmother I'm so quiet and sluggish I can barely speak. I don't see that they're pulling different things out of me—I just think that's how I am with them.

I'll spend the whole morning on my phone scrolling news and gossip, feeling scattered and anxious, then wonder why I'm so tired by lunchtime. It doesn't occur to me that what I'm putting into my mind is making me this way—I just think I slept badly.

My mother tells me to avoid certain friends because they make me restless, and she's right—after I see them I can't sit still and I'm rude to my sister. But I don't make the connection myself, I just feel wound up and don't know why.

Some evenings I come home from work and play with my son, laughing and fully present, other evenings I walk in and go straight to my room, too heavy to even greet him. I think it's just how my day went, not that I'm being carried by forces I'm not even watching.
